mainEntity.name: How do I get better at Street Photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: Pre-set exposure and zone focus, keep the camera ready, work good light and backgrounds, and wait for a gesture or interaction. Blend in, be patient, and shoot sets—not single frames.

mainEntity.name: What’s the number one rule of Street Photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: Respect people. Prioritise ethics and safety, de-escalate if challenged, and avoid photographing vulnerable subjects. Great street work balances candid honesty with empathy.

mainEntity.name: Is Street Photography legal in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: Generally yes from public places, but be respectful and follow site rules on private property or sensitive locations. For commercial use, consider model/property releases where appropriate.

mainEntity.name: What camera settings work well for Street Photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use 1/250–1/500s, Auto ISO with a sensible cap, and f/8–f/11 for zone focus. Shoot RAW, enable exposure compensation, and protect highlights in high-contrast light.

mainEntity.name: What is zone focusing and why use it?

acceptedAnswer.text: Zone focus pre-sets distance and aperture so a depth-of-field ‘zone’ is sharp. It removes AF lag and lets you shoot spontaneous moments fast and discreetly.

mainEntity.name: How do I handle confrontation when shooting on the street?

acceptedAnswer.text: Stay calm, explain your intent, and offer to move on. If needed, delete the frame to defuse tension. Your safety and respect for subjects come first.

mainEntity.name: How do I tell a story in Street Photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: Build mini-series around a theme—light, routine, commuters, or reflections. Use context (signage, architecture) and timing (gestures, glances) to connect people with place.
